hook: You already know how to measure angles in degrees and work with right triangles — but what happens when an angle is larger than 180°, or even negative? Trigonometry extends those familiar ideas into a powerful language that describes everything from sound waves and ocean tides to the spin of a turbine. In this unit, you'll discover how a simple circle unlocks the mathematics behind all of it.
concept: The unifying idea is that trigonometric functions translate circular motion and angles into predictable, repeating patterns — formally known as periodic functions — that model the world around us.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Angles and Their Measure (Degrees and Radians)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to fluently convert between degree and radian measure, setting up the precise language that all of trigonometry depends on.
Master the coordinates of key points on the unit circle so you can instantly recall exact trigonometric values at the most important angles.
You will extend sine, cosine, and the other trig functions beyond acute angles to any angle in any quadrant using reference angles and the unit circle.
Apply transformations — amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift — to accurately graph sine and cosine functions and interpret their behavior.
You will graph tangent, cotangent, secant, and cosecant, identifying their asymptotes and understanding how they relate to sine and cosine.
You will use inverse trigonometric functions to work backwards from a ratio to an angle, while carefully handling their restricted domains and ranges.
Apply sinusoidal functions to model real-world phenomena such as sound, springs, and tides, building equations that capture the amplitude, frequency, and phase of periodic motion.
